summary: Blazing Here Comes Sunshine jam, tight sets & strong improvisations
blurb: Energetic first set flows into a powerhouse second set highlighted by a soaring Here Comes Sunshine jam and an expansive Eyes Of The World. Jerry’s guitar is on fire while the band locks in tight transitions, delivering standout performances of China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider and Playing In The Band.
review: On April 2, 1973 the Grateful Dead took the Boston Garden by storm with a setlist that balanced concise rockers and soaring improvisations. The first set kept things tight—classic numbers like "The Promised Land", "Box of Rain" and a sparkling "China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider" transition—while the second set erupted after the opening "Here Comes Sunshine". Garcia’s blazing tone and adventurous solo turned the song into a 10‑minute jam that flowed seamlessly into an inspired "Playing In The Band" and a monumental 16‑minute "Eyes Of The World" that climaxed in the emotive "China Doll". Highlights also include the heartfelt "Jack Straw", the toe‑tapping "Loose Lucy", and the rousing encore "Johnny B. Goode".

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All five recordings are marked positive, repeatedly praising Jerry's blazing guitar, the epic Here Comes Sunshine jam, and the expansive Eyes Of The World. Multiple sources highlight the same standout songs and note tight band interplay, giving a strong consensus of excellence across the show.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with crystal‑clear mix, crisp instrument separation and the definitive Here Comes Sunshine jam.

key_highlights: Explosive Here Comes Sunshine jam that launches a superb second set.
"Eyes Of The World" stretches to 16 minutes, culminating in an emotional "China Doll".
Tight first‑set flow featuring a sparkling "China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider" transition.
High‑quality soundboard recording with clear mix and minimal technical flaws.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers blazing tone and exploratory solos, especially on Here Comes Sunshine, Eyes Of The World and the Greatest Story segment; his vocals are strong throughout.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides solid, melodic bass work, noted for nice interplay on Box of Rain and steady foundation during extended jams.
band_performance.Bob: Weir adds vocal depth on Looks Like Rain and energizes the crowd with his lead on Jack Straw.
band_performance.Keith: Keyboards shine with a space‑noodle on Here Comes Sunshine and tasteful chord transitions that enhance the jam sections.
